Q1: I'm pretty hands-on with how I learn, so my study routine usually involved a lot of practice problems and actually doing things rather than just reading. I'd set aside chunks of time, maybe around 22 hours a week, but I'd spread it out based on what felt right. I liked studying in the library or common areas where I could move around and take breaks. My routine wasn't super rigid—I'd adjust based on what assignments were coming up and how I was feeling that day.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, like studying most evenings, but deadlines would throw that off. When exams approached, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might slack a bit. I also have extracurriculars that take up time, so I had to be flexible. Honestly, I'd say it was more reactive than planned out perfectly.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed—like checking assignment details or downloading lecture slides. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that pushed me to check the LMS. I wasn't one to just browse around; I went in with a purpose and got out. If there was a deadline coming, that was definitely my main prompt to log in.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I checked automatically like social media or anything. I'd use it when I needed to, but it wasn't part of my daily habits. Maybe early in the semester I tried to check it more regularly, but that faded. It just felt like a tool rather than a space I wanted to spend time in.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful because I could pause and rewatch parts when I didn't get something the first time. I watched quite a lot of video content because it helped me understand concepts better than just reading. Being a kinesthetic learner, seeing things demonstrated helped me connect the dots. The discussion boards were less useful for me personally—I only posted a couple times.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the optional readings and some of the discussion prompts. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would actually help my grades. Some of the supplementary materials felt like extra work that wouldn't pay off directly. I also delayed some tasks because my assignment completion rate wasn't great—I focused more on exam prep since that's where I performed well.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. Practical exercises where I could apply concepts were valuable to me. If something felt too theoretical or abstract without clear application, I'd skip it. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff had this weight to it—I knew I had to do it or face consequences. Optional things? I'd look at them and think, 'Is this really going to help me?' With my anxiety about grades, I focused on what was mandatory first. Optional stuff usually got pushed aside unless I had extra time, which was rare.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline because I don't always have reliable internet access given my situation. I'd print things out when I could or save PDFs to my phone. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes found YouTube videos that explained things better than the course materials. A lot of my real studying happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper or reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with classmates in person—we'd quiz each other or work through practice problems together. That peer interaction was valuable but the LMS only captured a fraction of it. My exam scores reflect that learning even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline multiple times. I'd rewatch videos on my phone during commutes or breaks. The LMS would show I accessed it once, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline. It was just more practical given my limited resources.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and it became harder to focus on coursework. Sometimes when assignments piled up, I'd feel overwhelmed even though my stress levels are generally manageable. My extracurriculars also competed for attention, and there were weeks where I had to choose between activities. During those times, I'd do the minimum on the LMS and focus on what I could control.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, I'd use the LMS less frequently. I'd log in, grab what I needed quickly, and leave. Confusion about certain topics sometimes made me avoid the LMS because I'd rather figure things out through practice or asking classmates. My workload outside of academics meant I couldn't spend hours browsing course materials.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open a reading or start a video but my mind would be elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other things—anxious thoughts, stuff I needed to do later, whatever. I'd technically be 'accessing' the material but not really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, honestly. My assignment completion rate wasn't great because I'd prioritize exam prep over smaller tasks. Sometimes the anxiety made me avoid things that felt overwhelming—like if an assignment seemed confusing, I'd put it off hoping it would make more sense later. It's a bad habit but my resilience usually helped me push through eventually.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logging in more, downloading materials, checking requirements. Without a deadline, I'd drift away from the platform. It's not ideal but that pressure helped me focus. My response time to things averaged about a day, but that was usually deadline-motivated.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, especially when it was constructive. If I got good feedback on something, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. Negative feedback could spike my anxiety though, and sometimes I'd avoid checking grades for a while because of that. The CBT sessions I've been doing have helped me handle that better, but feedback still has an emotional impact on my engagement.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework, but I didn't always follow it closely. I appreciated knowing what was expected and when, but I often created my own path through the material based on what I needed for exams. A more flexible structure might have suited me better since I learn by doing rather than following a set sequence. The structure was helpful as a reference point though.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier because I could learn at my own pace. But some aspects made it harder—like when materials were only available online and I couldn't download them easily. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I naturally learn. More hands-on activities or practice problems would have engaged me better than reading-heavy content.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it, so they keep clicking around. Or maybe they're anxious like me and check things repeatedly without actually submitting work. It could also be that they're preparing in ways that don't translate to completed assignments—like they're learning but struggling with the final output. Life circumstances might also prevent them from finishing what they start.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. I had relatively low LMS engagement but scored really well on exams. I think it shows that learning happens in many ways—I studied offline, practiced problems on paper, and worked with classmates. The LMS was just one tool, not my main learning space. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are active and what they click on. They're good for tracking basic participation. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just scrolling. They miss offline study, emotional states, and the depth of understanding. My exam score versus my LMS activity is proof that logs don't capture everything.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement feels like being curious and invested in the outcome. It's doing practice problems until I can solve them without looking at notes. It's a mental state, not just an activity count.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how the CBT sessions have been helping me manage academic stress. It wouldn't capture the hours I spend studying offline with downloaded materials or the peer study sessions that really help me learn. It also wouldn't show my genuine happiness when I finally understand something difficult. My internal experience of learning is invisible to any tracking system.
